speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start by reviewing your recent lab work — your hemoglobin level came back at 92 g/L, which is below the normal range.

speaker: patient
text: Oh, that’s lower than last time. I’ve been feeling more tired lately, especially when walking up stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, and your diastolic blood pressure today is 68 mmHg — on the lower side, especially given your history. How have you been feeling with dizziness or lightheadedness?

speaker: patient
text: A little dizzy when standing up quickly, yes — and my ankles have been swelling more over the past week.

speaker: doctor
text: That aligns with your established diagnosis of Coronary Artery Disease — we’ll need to assess whether this is worsening heart function contributing to fluid retention.

speaker: patient
text: Exactly — the lower limb edema has gotten worse since last Thursday. It’s mostly in both ankles, and it doesn’t go down much even after elevating them overnight.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is currently 90 bpm — slightly elevated for resting, which may reflect compensatory effort given the anemia and possible decompensation.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also noticed my pulse feels stronger than usual, especially in my neck — like it’s pounding sometimes.

speaker: doctor
text: Your body temperature is 36.1 °C — well within normal limits, so no sign of acute infection contributing to this picture.

speaker: patient
text: No fever, no chills — just fatigue, shortness of breath on exertion, and this persistent swelling.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the low hemoglobin, borderline low diastolic pressure, elevated heart rate, and new-onset edema, I’m concerned about early heart failure exacerbation. Let’s order a BNP and repeat ferritin studies.

speaker: patient
text: I remember my hemoglobin was 104 g/L three months ago — so it’s dropped quite a bit. Could the iron pills I started help?

speaker: doctor
text: That’s right — the drop from 104 g/L to 92 g/L is significant, and suggests either ongoing blood loss or inadequate absorption. We’ll check for GI sources and consider IV iron if oral isn’t sufficient.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked again this morning before coming in, my blood pressure was 118 over 72 — so the diastolic was a bit higher than what you just recorded.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for that update — the 68 mmHg reading was taken after you’d been sitting quietly for five minutes, but home readings can vary. We’ll monitor trends closely.

speaker: patient
text: And the swelling — it’s worse in the evenings, and my rings feel tight by dinner time. That number from last time — the 92 g/L — makes me wonder if it’s related.

speaker: doctor
text: It absolutely could be — anemia reduces oxygen-carrying capacity, forcing the heart to work harder, which over time can worsen fluid retention in patients with CAD.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also been taking lisinopril 10 mg daily, but my cardiologist mentioned possibly adjusting it. Is that still appropriate?

speaker: doctor
text: Given your current low diastolic pressure and signs of volume overload, I am going to switch you to sacubitril/valsartan instead — it’s better tolerated in this context and improves outcomes in HFrEF.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — thank you. I’ll bring my home BP log next time, and let you know how the swelling responds over the next week.
